name: General Magic Telescript
short_description: The first commercial mobile-agent platform: software agents carrying 'work orders' traveled across network nodes to execute tasks and return results — an explicit pre-web tool-call architecture.
long_description: Telescript, developed by General Magic starting in 1991 and first publicly demonstrated at Macworld Expo in January 1994, was an object-oriented language and runtime for mobile software agents. Rather than keeping a live connection open, a Telescript agent carried its complete task specification to a remote 'place' on the network, executed there (booking a flight, checking stock prices, comparing retail offers), and returned only the result — reducing every distributed operation to a single 'go' instruction. The platform ran on dedicated server infrastructure operated by AT&T, Sony, NTT, and France Telecom, and shipped on consumer hardware via the Sony Magic Link PDA and Motorola Envoy in 1994. Hardware and carrier adoption remained limited, but the architecture anticipated cloud-executed agentic tool-calling by three decades.
ai_note: Telescript agents are deterministic procedural programs, not ML models; intelligence is the network's routing and agent-dispatch logic, not learned inference.

images.caption: The Sony Magic Link PIC-1000, the consumer PDA that shipped Telescript and Magic Cap to the public in 1994.
images.alt_text: A Sony Magic Link PIC-1000 personal digital assistant lying flat, showing its landscape touchscreen displaying the Magic Cap desktop interface with small room-like icons.

sources.title: General Magic
sources.access_notes: Company history, founding 1990, product timeline, Magic Cap and Telescript overview, General Magic Alliance partners.

sources.title: Telescript (programming language)
sources.access_notes: Technical overview: mobile agents, places, 'go' instruction, cross-platform bytecode, cloud execution model, comparison with Java applets.

sources.title: Past Futures of Programming: General Magic's Telescript
sources.access_notes: Technical deep-dive on Telescript's places/agents/travel metaphor, 'go' instruction semantics, use cases (ticket booking, price monitoring, camera shopping), and comparison with RPC.

sources.title: 1994 Software Agents List Archive: Info on Telescript
sources.access_notes: Primary 1994 mailing list archive describing Telescript engine, Places and Agents class hierarchy, agent migration mechanism, and permit/authority security model.

interactions.title: Send an agent to find the cheapest camera price
interactions.description: A user dispatches a Telescript agent with a product name and a maximum price; the agent visits multiple virtual storefronts in the network, compares prices, and returns only the winning offer — completing in fifteen minutes what a phone call to each retailer would have taken all day.
interactions.source_label: Instadeq — Past Futures of Programming: Telescript

interactions.title: Book a theater ticket without staying connected
interactions.description: Rather than keeping an open phone line, a Telescript agent travels to the theater's 'place' on the network, checks seat availability, negotiates the transaction, and returns a confirmation — the first commercial example of a software agent carrying a complete work order to a remote endpoint.
interactions.source_label: Instadeq — Past Futures of Programming: Telescript

interactions.title: A price-watcher that never logs off
interactions.description: An agent is given a target price and left running on a remote 'place'; it monitors the product until the price drops to the threshold, then sends a result back to the user's device — a persistent background agent managing a condition on the user's behalf without any ongoing connection.
interactions.source_label: Instadeq — Past Futures of Programming: Telescript

interactions.title: One spoken command fans out across services
interactions.description: Because Telescript's 'go' instruction let an agent chain through places, a single request from the Magic Link could trigger a cascade — check the calendar, book a table, confirm a reminder — each step handled by a specialized place on the network, foreshadowing modern multi-step LLM tool-call chains.
